title: Git Command Cheat Sheet
content: A quick Git reference covering basic, advanced, daily, interview, and high-risk workflows.

detail.usage: Initialize a new Git repository in the current directory
detail.example: mkdir my-project && cd my-project && git init
detail.notes: Creates hidden .git directory

detail.usage: Copy entire remote repository to local
detail.notes: Clones all branch history by default

items.desc: Check working directory and staging area status

detail.usage: View current branch, staged files, unstaged files and untracked files
detail.notes: Most commonly used to check status before commit

detail.usage: Add modifications of specified files or directories to staging area
detail.example: git add src/main.js or git add .
detail.notes: git add -p enables interactive selection of content to stage

detail.usage: Commit staging area content to local repository
detail.notes: Clear commit messages are recommended

detail.usage: View commit records sorted in reverse chronological order
detail.notes: Ideal for viewing full author, timestamp and change summary

items.desc: View difference between working directory and staging area

detail.usage: Compare unstaged changes against staging area
detail.notes: git diff --cached shows difference between staging area and latest commit

detail.usage: Fetch remote branch updates and merge into current branch
detail.notes: Equivalent to git fetch + git merge

detail.usage: Sync new commits from current branch to remote repository
detail.notes: First push often uses -u to set upstream branch

detail.usage: View existing local branches and mark current active branch
detail.notes: git branch -a also shows remote branches

detail.usage: Create a new branch based on current HEAD
detail.notes: Does not switch to the new branch automatically after creation

detail.usage: Skip git add, directly commit changes to Git-tracked files
detail.notes: Does not include new untracked files

detail.usage: Switch to specified branch to continue development
detail.notes: Commonly used in older Git versions; newer versions can also use git switch

items.desc: Create and switch to new branch

detail.usage: Create new branch and switch to it immediately
detail.notes: Equivalent to git branch + git checkout

detail.usage: Delete local branches that have been developed and merged
detail.notes: Use -D to force delete unmerged branches

detail.usage: Display each commit in one line for quick history browsing
detail.notes: --graph renders branch diagram

items.desc: View difference between working directory and latest commit

detail.usage: Compare overall working directory state against latest commit
detail.notes: Includes both staged and unstaged changes

detail.usage: View remote repository aliases and their fetch/push addresses
detail.notes: Very useful for troubleshooting which repository you are pushing to

detail.usage: Merge commits from specified branch into current branch
detail.example: git checkout main && git merge dev
detail.notes: Resolve conflicts manually first, then run git add + git commit

items.desc: Rebase current branch onto target branch

detail.usage: Reapply commits from current branch after latest commit of target branch
detail.notes: Avoid rebase on public branches as it rewrites history

detail.usage: Temporarily save uncommitted changes to switch tasks or branches easily
detail.notes: git stash list shows all stash records

detail.usage: Restore the most recent stash and delete that record
detail.notes: git stash apply restores the stash without deleting the record

detail.usage: Apply a single commit from another branch to current branch
detail.notes: Ideal for picking a single bugfix without merging the entire branch

detail.usage: View, add or manage submodules in the main repository
detail.notes: Submodules track specific commits, not branch states

detail.usage: Quickly locate which commit introduced a bug via binary search
detail.notes: Requires good/bad markers to narrow down scope gradually

detail.usage: Batch modify repository history, e.g. rewrite author info or delete sensitive files
detail.notes: Rewrites entire history, back up before execution. git filter-repo is recommended for modern use cases

detail.usage: Check out multiple branches to different directories from the same repository for parallel work
detail.notes: Ideal for parallel hotfix development and mainline work

detail.usage: View movement records of HEAD and branch references
detail.notes: Extremely useful for recovering lost commits after reset or rebase

detail.usage: Export archive package without .git history based on a specified commit
detail.notes: Ideal for packaging source code snapshots for release

detail.usage: Aggregate commit records by author, suitable for contribution statistics
detail.notes: -s shows commit count, -n sorts by commit count

items.desc: Understand differences between merge and rebase

detail.usage: merge preserves fork history, rebase rewrites commit base for more linear history
detail.example: git checkout main && git merge feature / git checkout feature && git rebase main
detail.notes: Unify conventions for team collaboration; avoid rebase on public branches as a general rule

detail.usage: --soft only moves HEAD; --mixed resets staging area; --hard discards working directory changes at the same time
detail.notes: Default mode is --mixed, --hard carries highest risk

detail.usage: Generate a new reverse commit to undo the effects of the specified commit
detail.notes: Suitable for commits already pushed to remote, does not rewrite history

detail.usage: Delete files or directories in working directory not tracked by Git
detail.notes: Always use -n to preview first before deciding to delete

detail.usage: Tag specific commits, often used for version releases
detail.example: git tag v1.0.0 && git push origin v1.0.0
detail.notes: git tag -a creates annotated tags

detail.usage: Set username, email, editor, aliases and other Git configurations
detail.notes: --global applies to current user, omit to apply only to current repository

detail.usage: List file paths tracked or staged by Git
detail.notes: Often used in scripts or for troubleshooting index status

items.desc: Show detailed information of a commit

detail.usage: View author, timestamp, description and diff content of a given commit
detail.notes: You can also use git show <tag> to view the commit corresponding to a tag

detail.usage: Check for corrupted or dangling objects in object database and references
detail.notes: Usually used for troubleshooting repository anomalies or history corruption

detail.usage: Rollback to previous commit, discard both working directory and staging area changes
detail.notes: Dangerous operation, unsaved changes may be unrecoverable

detail.usage: Ignore remote history check, directly overwrite remote branch with local history
detail.notes: May overwrite others' commits; safer alternative is git push --force-with-lease

detail.usage: Delete untracked files directly without confirmation prompts
detail.notes: Irreversible, run git clean -n to preview first before execution

detail.usage: Modify message of most recent commit or add missing files
detail.notes: If the commit has been pushed, amend usually requires force push afterwards

detail.usage: Delete specified or most recent stash record
detail.notes: Usually unrecoverable after deletion, confirm you no longer need it before execution

detail.usage: Perform merge and leave result in staged state, waiting for manual check and commit
detail.notes: Suitable for scenarios where merge results need manual confirmation

detail.usage: Initialize and check out submodules to recorded commit after cloning repository
detail.notes: Submodule state may change after switching branches, verify submodule pointers in parent repository first

detail.usage: Clean up expired reference records in reflog per rules
detail.notes: Reduces opportunity to recover historical operations after cleanup, usually only used in maintenance scenarios

title: npm / pnpm Command Cheat Sheet
content: A quick npm / pnpm reference covering beginner, advanced, daily, interview, and failure-prone package workflows.

detail.usage: Create a new package.json as the entry manifest for project dependencies and scripts
detail.notes: Use with -y to quickly generate default configuration

detail.usage: Install current project dependencies based on package.json and lock file
detail.notes: pnpm stores packages in a global store then links them to the project directory

detail.usage: Add the specified package to dependencies and install it to the project
detail.example: npm install axios 或 pnpm add axios
detail.notes: npm uses install for single packages, pnpm uses add instead

detail.usage: Remove the specified dependency from node_modules, package.json and lock file
detail.example: npm uninstall axios 或 pnpm remove axios
detail.notes: Confirm it is not a core runtime dependency before removal

detail.usage: Execute scripts.start defined in package.json
detail.notes: Suitable for launching production services or preview entry points

detail.usage: Execute any script defined in package.json
detail.example: npm run build / pnpm run dev
detail.notes: npm allows omitting run for start and test, other scripts usually require it

detail.usage: View installed dependencies and their hierarchical structure in the project
detail.notes: Very useful for troubleshooting version conflicts

detail.usage: Check current version of local npm or pnpm CLI
detail.notes: Use --version or -v to check tool version, not npm version

items.cmd: npm config get registry / pnpm config get registry

detail.usage: Read current registry configuration, confirm if official source or company private registry is used
detail.example: npm config get registry / pnpm config get registry
detail.notes: Confirm after switching registry before installing dependencies

detail.usage: Install CLI tools to global environment for reuse across multiple projects
detail.example: npm install -g vite / pnpm add -g vite
detail.notes: Global installation can cause version drift; team projects are recommended to use local dependencies with npx or pnpm dlx

detail.usage: View command descriptions, parameters and examples
detail.example: npm help install / pnpm help add
detail.notes: Check help when you forget commands, it is safer than guessing parameters

detail.usage: Temporarily pull and run commands provided by npm packages without global installation
detail.example: npx create-vite my-app / pnpm dlx create-vite my-app
detail.notes: Suitable for scaffolding, one-time generators and short-term tools

items.desc: Temporarily install without writing to package.json

detail.usage: Only install to current node_modules, do not modify dependency manifest
detail.notes: pnpm 10.x official docs do not provide a fully equivalent add --no-save option

detail.usage: Install only devDependencies, suitable for toolchain analysis or dev container scenarios
detail.notes: Older references often use npm install --only=dev; current npm recommends omit/include semantics

detail.usage: Ignore devDependencies, install only runtime required dependencies
detail.notes: Commonly used for deployment images and production environments

detail.usage: Link a locally developed library to another project for joint debugging
detail.example: cd lib && npm link；cd app && npm link lib
detail.notes: Newer pnpm versions recommend explicitly passing directory paths to avoid ambiguous link behavior

detail.usage: Clean up packages in node_modules that are not declared in the manifest or no longer needed
detail.notes: pnpm prune can also be used with --prod or --no-optional for trimming

detail.usage: Scan for known vulnerabilities in the current dependency tree
detail.notes: After finding issues, evaluate impact first before deciding to update, override or fix

items.desc: Stable installation based on lock file

detail.usage: Install dependencies strictly according to lockfile, avoid version drift in pipelines
detail.notes: pnpm 10.x commonly uses frozen-lockfile; pnpm 11 introduces pnpm ci

detail.usage: List current version, available version and latest version
detail.notes: Check differences before upgrading, safer than running update directly

detail.usage: Publish the current package to npm registry or private source
detail.notes: Check name, version, access, files and registry before publishing to avoid accidental releases

detail.usage: Disable funding prompts during installation to reduce CI log noise
detail.notes: pnpm 10.x official CLI docs do not provide a fully equivalent parameter with the same name

items.desc: Update dependencies to the latest allowed version

detail.usage: Update project dependencies according to semver ranges
detail.notes: May seem like a minor change, but can still trigger behavior changes; check changelog before upgrading

items.cmd: npm run build / pnpm run build

detail.usage: Execute the build artifact output process
detail.example: npm run build / pnpm run build
detail.notes: Commonly used before release, local validation and CI

items.cmd: npm run dev / pnpm run dev

detail.usage: Start local development mode, hot reload or watch process
detail.example: npm run dev / pnpm run dev
detail.notes: Most common in frontend and toolchain projects

items.cmd: npm cache clean --force / pnpm store prune

detail.usage: Clear npm cache, or clean unreferenced packages in pnpm global store
detail.example: npm cache clean --force / pnpm store prune
detail.notes: Subsequent installations may require re-downloading dependencies after cleaning

detail.usage: Add tooling dependencies to devDependencies
detail.example: npm install -D typescript / pnpm add -D typescript
detail.notes: Compilers, test frameworks, lint tools are usually placed in devDependencies

detail.usage: Write dependency as exact version number, avoid auto drift caused by ^ or ~
detail.example: npm install --save-exact react@19.0.0 / pnpm add --save-exact react@19.0.0
detail.notes: Suitable for infrastructure projects requiring strict version locking

items.desc: Ignore peer dependency conflicts and continue installation

detail.usage: Force completion of npm installation when peerDependencies conflict
detail.notes: pnpm does not have an identical parameter with the same name; prioritize fixing peer versions over long-term bypass of checks

detail.usage: Write authentication information for publishing, installing private packages or accessing private sources
detail.notes: pnpm login capability varies by version; older versions may rely on npm compatibility process, newer versions are gradually natively implemented

detail.usage: Clear login state or authentication information for current registry
detail.notes: Recommended to log out promptly after switching registries or using shared machines

items.desc: Understand the difference between the two dependency installation mechanisms

detail.usage: npm defaults to hoisted node_modules structure, pnpm uses a global content-addressed store and reuses packages via hard/symbolic links
detail.notes: pnpm usually saves more disk space and is better for sharing dependency cache across multiple projects

items.desc: Understand the difference between CI installation and regular installation

detail.usage: ci requires strict lockfile consistency and will not update lockfile; install is more flexible and may complete or update lockfile
detail.example: npm ci 用于流水线，npm install 用于本地开发
detail.notes: If lockfile and package.json are inconsistent, npm ci will fail directly

detail.usage: Automatically attempt to upgrade or adjust dependencies based on security reports
detail.notes: Check diff before and after auto-fix to confirm no breaking changes are introduced

items.cmd: npm config set registry <url> / pnpm config set registry <url>

detail.usage: Change default registry to official source, mirror site or company private registry
detail.example: npm config set registry https://registry.npmjs.org/ / pnpm config set registry https://registry.npmjs.org/
detail.notes: After switching registry, better confirm with config get registry again

detail.usage: Make npm install dependencies with a shallow directory structure closer to global installation
detail.notes: npm 11 has marked this parameter as deprecated, recommend using --install-strategy=shallow instead

detail.usage: Skip optionalDependencies during installation to reduce size or avoid platform-related dependencies
detail.notes: Suitable for CI, containers or scenarios sensitive to installation size

detail.usage: Ignore some protection checks and force continuation of installation process
detail.notes: May hide real dependency issues, only recommended for temporary troubleshooting

items.desc: Risk of accidental deletion of critical dependencies

detail.usage: Uninstall command modifies both dependency manifest and lock file
detail.example: npm uninstall react / pnpm remove react
detail.notes: Accidental deletion of runtime dependencies may cause the project to fail to start directly

items.desc: Version chaos caused by global installation

detail.usage: When multiple projects reuse the same global tool, versions may pollute each other
detail.example: npm install -g eslint / pnpm add -g eslint
detail.notes: For team unified environments, local dependencies or locked executors are more recommended

items.desc: Dependency loss caused by forgetting to write to dependency manifest

detail.usage: Runs on current machine, but may lack packages after reinstallation on other machines
detail.notes: Unless it is explicitly a temporary experiment, do not use --no-save as a regular installation method

items.desc: Accidental installation of only dev dependencies in production

detail.usage: If production or build environment only installs devDependencies, runtime dependencies will be missing
detail.notes: Distinguish stages between build and runtime containers, do not mix parameters

items.desc: Risk of API incompatibility after upgrade

detail.usage: Even if semver is ostensibly followed, behavior changes or ecosystem compatibility issues may occur
detail.notes: Check changelog before upgrade, run tests and build at minimum after upgrade

items.desc: Unremoved local links affect other projects

detail.usage: link changes dependency resolution paths, may affect subsequent projects if not cleaned up after joint debugging
detail.notes: Remember to unlink after debugging and reinstall official dependencies

items.cmd: npm cache clean --force / pnpm store prune
items.desc: Re-download of dependencies required after cleaning

detail.usage: After cache and store are cleared, next installation is usually slower
detail.example: npm cache clean --force / pnpm store prune
detail.notes: Not a regular speed-up measure, only worth executing when cache is corrupted or space needs to be freed

items.desc: Ignoring peer dependencies may cause runtime errors

detail.usage: Although installation can complete first, real issues may be delayed until build or runtime
detail.notes: The safer approach is to align peer versions or upgrade related plugin chains

items.desc: Accidental publication of private packages or wrong versions

detail.usage: Once publish command executes successfully, wrong content may be synced to public repository
detail.notes: Do final confirmation with pack, dry-run, version check and registry check before publishing

title: JavaScript Array Methods
content: A JavaScript array reference spanning basic operations, iteration, search, and modern immutable APIs.

items.desc: Append elements to the end of the array

detail.usage: Add one or more elements to the end of the array, return the new length
detail.notes: Mutates the original array

detail.usage: Delete the last element of the array, return the removed value
detail.notes: Mutates the original array; returns undefined for empty arrays

detail.usage: Delete the first element of the array, return the removed element
detail.notes: Mutates the original array; high performance cost for frequent use on large arrays

items.desc: Insert elements to the start of the array

detail.usage: Insert one or more elements to the head of the array, return the new length
detail.notes: Mutates the original array

items.desc: Merge arrays and return a new array

detail.usage: Concatenate two or more arrays into a single new array
detail.notes: Does not mutate the original array

items.desc: Concatenate array elements into a string

detail.usage: Join array elements into a string with the specified separator
detail.notes: Default separator is comma

detail.usage: Reverse the order of array elements in place
detail.notes: Mutates the original array; use toReversed() to avoid mutation

detail.usage: Sort the array in place using the provided compare function
detail.notes: Defaults to string comparison; mutates the original array

detail.usage: Return a new array for the specified range, excludes the end position
detail.notes: Does not mutate the original array; often used for shallow copy

detail.usage: Delete, insert or replace elements at the specified index
detail.notes: Mutates the original array; returns an array of deleted elements

detail.usage: Execute the callback function once for each element
detail.notes: No return value; not suitable for mid-iteration break

detail.usage: Execute function on each element and generate a new array
detail.notes: Does not mutate the original array

detail.usage: Return a new array consisting of elements that meet the condition
detail.notes: Does not mutate the original array

detail.usage: Return the first element that meets the condition
detail.notes: Returns undefined if no match is found

items.desc: Find index of first matching element

detail.usage: Return the index of the first element that meets the condition
detail.notes: Returns -1 if no match is found

detail.usage: Returns true if at least one element meets the condition
detail.notes: Short-circuits and returns immediately when first match is found

detail.usage: Returns true if all elements meet the condition
detail.notes: Returns true for empty arrays

detail.usage: Accumulate array values into a single result
detail.notes: Always provide an initial value to avoid edge case behavior

items.desc: Reduce array from right to left

detail.usage: Execute reduce logic starting from the right side of the array
detail.notes: Useful for function composition or right-associative scenarios

detail.usage: Equivalent to map followed by flat(1), often used for one-to-many transformations
detail.notes: Only flattens 1 level deep

detail.usage: Return the index of the first occurrence of the specified element
detail.notes: Returns -1 if not found; uses strict equality comparison

detail.usage: Search for element from right to left and return its index
detail.notes: Returns -1 if not found

items.desc: Check if specified element is present

detail.usage: Check if a value exists in the array
detail.notes: More intuitive than indexOf, and correctly identifies NaN

detail.usage: Search from right to left for the first element that meets the condition
detail.notes: Suitable for reverse search of the latest matching result

items.desc: Find index of last matching element

detail.usage: Search from right to left for the index of the element that meets the condition
detail.notes: Returns -1 if no match is found

detail.usage: Get the iterator object for array indices
detail.notes: Often used with for...of or spread operator

detail.usage: Get the iterator object for array element values
detail.notes: for...of iterates over array values by default, same semantics as values()

detail.usage: Generate iterator in [index, value] format
detail.notes: Suitable for getting both index and value at the same time

detail.usage: Replace elements in the specified range of the array with the same value
detail.notes: Mutates the original array

detail.usage: Copy a segment of the array to another position in the same array
detail.notes: Mutates the original array, but does not change its length

detail.usage: Flatten nested array by the specified depth
detail.notes: Use Infinity to flatten completely

detail.usage: Return a new array with elements in reverse order of the original
detail.notes: Does not mutate the original array; immutable version of reverse()

detail.usage: Sort the array and return a new array, does not modify the original array
detail.notes: Suitable for scenarios relying on immutable data such as React

detail.usage: Perform splice-like insert and delete operations, but return a new array
detail.notes: Does not mutate the original array

items.desc: Return new array with specified index replaced

detail.usage: Replace the value at the specified index and return a new array
detail.notes: Supports negative indices; does not mutate the original array

detail.usage: Get element at specified position, supports negative indices
detail.notes: More concise than arr[arr.length - 1]

detail.usage: Group array elements into object properties based on callback result
detail.notes: Current standard syntax is Object.groupBy / Map.groupBy; early Array.prototype.group proposal has been revised

title: CSS Flexbox Reference
content: Core properties and layout mental models for CSS Flexbox.

detail.usage: Set the element as a flex container
detail.notes: inline-flex creates an inline-level flex container

detail.usage: Control alignment of child elements on the main axis
detail.notes: Allowed values: flex-start/end/center/space-between/around/evenly

detail.usage: Control alignment of child elements on the cross axis
detail.notes: Default value stretch stretches child elements

detail.usage: Change the direction of the main axis
detail.notes: Main axis becomes vertical when set to column

detail.usage: Auto-wrap child elements when they overflow the container
detail.notes: Default nowrap disables line wrapping

detail.usage: Set the gap between child elements
detail.notes: Simpler alternative to margin-based spacing solutions

items.desc: Split available space equally among children

detail.usage: flex: 1 is equivalent to flex: 1 1 0%
detail.notes: Shorthand for flex-grow:1 flex-shrink:1 flex-basis:0%

detail.usage: Override the parent container's align-items setting
detail.notes: Only affects the current element

title: Linux / Shell Commands
content: A Linux / Shell reference covering daily commands, permissions, processes, networking, and scripting.

detail.usage: View files and subdirectories, -l shows detailed info, -a shows hidden files
detail.notes: -h renders file sizes in human-readable format

detail.usage: Switch the shell's active working directory
detail.notes: cd - returns to the previous working directory

detail.usage: Output the absolute path of the current directory
detail.notes: Useful for troubleshooting relative path issues

detail.usage: Create new directories, -p recursively creates missing parent directories
detail.notes: -p suppresses errors if the target directory already exists

detail.usage: Delete files, use -r to recursively delete directories
detail.notes: rm -rf is extremely dangerous, confirm the path before execution

detail.usage: Copy single files, use -r to recursively copy directory trees
detail.notes: Existing target files will be overwritten

detail.usage: Move files to a new location, or rename items in the same directory
detail.notes: Most commonly used for renaming items in the same directory

items.desc: Create empty files or update timestamps

detail.usage: Create new empty files, or update modification time of existing files
detail.notes: Widely used in scripts to generate placeholder files

detail.usage: Output file contents, or concatenate multiple files
detail.notes: Use less for viewing long files

detail.usage: Output plain text, variable values, or redirect output to files
detail.notes: Often used with > or >> to write to files

detail.usage: Set read/write/execute permissions, 755 is common for scripts and directories
detail.notes: Numeric permissions: 7 = rwx, 5 = r-x

detail.usage: Adjust owner and group of files or directories
detail.notes: Usually requires root privileges

detail.usage: Only change the file's group, leave owner unchanged
detail.notes: Ideal for permission adjustment on shared directories

detail.usage: View permission bits, owner, size and modification time
detail.notes: First column (e.g. -rwxr-xr-x) shows permission info

detail.usage: Search files by name, size, time, type and other rules
detail.notes: Combine flags like -type, -mtime, -size for complex queries

detail.usage: Match text in files, -r recursively searches directories
detail.notes: -n shows line numbers, -i ignores case

detail.usage: Archive files/directories with tar and compress via gzip
detail.notes: Common decompression command: tar -xzvf archive.tar.gz

detail.usage: Compress single files to gzip format
detail.notes: Replaces original file with .gz file by default

detail.usage: Compress files to .bz2 format
detail.notes: Offers higher compression ratio, but slower speed

detail.usage: Extract zip archives to current or specified directory
detail.notes: Ideal for handling cross-platform zip packages

detail.usage: Dynamically monitor CPU, memory usage and process status
detail.notes: htop is a more modern alternative, requires separate installation

detail.usage: List all running processes on the system
detail.notes: Often combined with grep to filter target processes

detail.usage: Send signals to processes, -9 forces immediate termination
detail.notes: Try default TERM or -15 first, -9 kills processes with no cleanup

detail.usage: View, start, stop or restart systemd services
detail.notes: Common actions: start / stop / restart / status / enable

detail.usage: Show total, used and free space for disk partitions
detail.notes: -h outputs values in human-readable format

detail.usage: Calculate space occupied by files or directories
detail.notes: Combine with sort to locate large directories

detail.usage: View memory and swap space usage
detail.notes: Common Linux command for quick memory pressure assessment

detail.usage: Show system uptime and load averages
detail.notes: Allows quick check of recent load averages

detail.usage: List users logged in to current system sessions
detail.notes: Useful for multi-user servers

detail.usage: Read wtmp logs to show historical login records
detail.notes: Ideal for auditing recent server access

detail.usage: Send ICMP requests to target host to check connectivity
detail.notes: ICMP may be blocked in some networks, failure does not mean service is down

detail.usage: Show NIC addresses and network interface information
detail.notes: Most modern distros recommend the ip command by default

detail.usage: Manage IP addresses, routes and link information
detail.example: ip addr show or ip route
detail.notes: Modern replacement for ifconfig / route

detail.usage: Show listening ports, connection status and associated processes
detail.notes: ss is recommended as an alternative on some systems

detail.usage: Test endpoints, download responses or debug APIs from command line
detail.notes: -s enables silent mode, -o writes output to file

detail.usage: Download files from remote URLs to local storage
detail.notes: Ideal for scripted resource downloads

detail.usage: Log in to remote hosts via SSH for administration
detail.notes: Use key-based authentication instead of passwords for production

items.desc: Securely copy files to remote hosts

detail.usage: Copy files between local and remote hosts via SSH protocol
detail.notes: Add -r for recursive directory copy

detail.usage: Quickly verify if a TCP port is reachable
detail.notes: nc or curl are common alternatives in modern environments

detail.usage: Show network hops packets take to reach target host
detail.notes: Helpful for troubleshooting network link issues

detail.usage: Parse text by columns, perform pattern matching, calculation and formatted output
detail.notes: Extremely powerful for processing structured plain text

detail.usage: Replace, delete or modify text streams without opening an editor
detail.notes: -i modifies files directly, confirm before execution

detail.usage: Enable extended regular expressions for complex text matching
detail.notes: Ideal for matching multiple patterns in one command

detail.usage: Execute command on each file matched by find
detail.notes: Preview results by removing -exec before bulk delete/move

detail.usage: Concatenate previous command output into argument list for next command
detail.notes: Use -0 with null separators for paths containing spaces

detail.usage: Edit cron scheduled tasks for current user
detail.notes: Configure cron via crontab instead of running cron directly

detail.usage: Execute script files with Bash interpreter
detail.notes: Scripts often start with shebang line #!/usr/bin/env bash

detail.usage: Apply script changes in current shell context
detail.notes: Often used to reload environment variable configurations

detail.usage: Define exit, cleanup and exception handling logic for shell scripts
detail.notes: Useful for writing robust scripts

detail.usage: Pass string back to shell for interpretation and execution
detail.notes: Carries command injection risk, avoid using unless necessary

title: Windows cmd Commands
content: A Windows cmd reference for core commands, filesystem work, network diagnostics, batch scripts, and handy tricks.

detail.usage: View the list of files and folders in the current directory
detail.notes: /a shows hidden files, equivalent to ls -a on Linux

detail.usage: Switch the working directory in the command line
detail.notes: Use the /d flag when switching across different drives

detail.usage: Create one or more new directories
detail.notes: mkdir supports creating nested directories directly

detail.usage: Delete directories; /s recursively deletes all subdirectories and files
detail.notes: /q enables silent mode which carries high risk, verify the path before execution

detail.usage: Copy one or more files to the target location
detail.notes: Use xcopy or robocopy for more complex directory copy tasks

detail.usage: Move files to another directory, or rename files in the same directory
detail.notes: May overwrite existing files at the target path

detail.usage: Delete one or more files
detail.notes: Verify the matching range before batch deleting with wildcards

detail.usage: Print the content of a text file to the console
detail.notes: Suitable for short text; use a paging tool for long files

detail.usage: Print strings, or echo the value of environment variables
detail.notes: Often used with redirection to generate batch files or configuration content

detail.usage: Clear the content of the current command line window
detail.notes: Only clears the display, does not affect command history

detail.usage: View or set read-only, hidden, system and other attributes
detail.notes: Commonly used to hide files or remove read-only attributes

detail.usage: Print differences between two text or binary files
detail.notes: Convenient for simple diff use cases

detail.usage: Modify the name of a file or directory

detail.usage: View current time, or adjust manually with sufficient permissions
detail.notes: /t only displays the time without triggering modification prompt

detail.usage: View OS version, patches, boot time, memory and other system information
detail.notes: Very helpful for troubleshooting machine environment issues

detail.usage: List all currently running processes on the system

detail.usage: Terminate the process matching the specified PID or image name
detail.notes: /f forces process termination, use with caution

detail.usage: Perform system operations such as shutdown, restart, logoff, etc.
detail.notes: /s shuts down, /r restarts, /t specifies delay in seconds

detail.notes: System disk check may require a reboot to run

detail.usage: Send ICMP requests to target host to check reachability

detail.usage: View local IP, gateway, DNS and other network information
detail.notes: Commonly used for troubleshooting DHCP, DNS and network adapter configuration issues

detail.usage: View the hop route to the target host
detail.notes: Equivalent to traceroute on Linux

items.desc: View network connections and port status

detail.usage: View listening ports, connection status and associated PID

detail.usage: Display local ARP table entries, view IP to MAC address mappings
detail.notes: Helpful for local area network troubleshooting

detail.usage: Query domain name resolution records and DNS server responses

detail.usage: Verify if the specified TCP port is reachable
detail.notes: Telnet client may need to be enabled separately on Windows

detail.usage: Connect to FTP server to upload and download files
detail.notes: SFTP or SCP is more commonly used in modern scenarios

detail.usage: View current routing rules, or add/delete static routes
detail.notes: Commonly used for multi-egress network or VPN troubleshooting

detail.usage: Configure network interfaces, firewall, Wi-Fi, proxy and other system network settings
detail.notes: Powerful but command syntax is lengthy, suitable for scripts and O&M scenarios

detail.usage: Iterate over files, numbers or command output in batch scripts
detail.notes: Use %i in command line, %%i in batch files

detail.usage: Execute logic based on error code, string comparison or file existence

detail.usage: Jump to the specified label inside a batch script
detail.notes: Overuse reduces script readability

detail.usage: Execute another batch script and return to continue running the current script
detail.notes: If executed directly without call, the current script may exit early

detail.usage: Create, modify or view environment variables
detail.notes: Takes effect in the current window only; use setx for permanent variables

detail.usage: Shifts %1 to the original %2, used to process multiple parameters sequentially
detail.notes: Useful for parsing batch scripts with multiple parameters

items.desc: Exit command line or current script

detail.usage: Exit the current cmd session, or terminate the current batch script
detail.notes: /b is commonly used to return an error code without closing the entire window

detail.usage: Search for string or regex pattern in files or command output
detail.notes: Equivalent to grep in Windows command line

detail.usage: Recursively copy directories and subfiles
detail.notes: Robocopy is recommended for complex copy tasks on modern Windows

detail.notes: More stable than xcopy for O&M and backup scenarios

detail.usage: Escape special characters such as &, |, >, etc.
detail.notes: Very common when handling special symbols in batch scripts

detail.usage: Read the value of the current environment variable
detail.notes: Suitable for regular command line or scripts without delayed expansion enabled

detail.usage: Read real-time updated variable values in loops or conditional blocks
detail.notes: Delayed expansion must be enabled first

detail.usage: Pass the output of the previous command as input to the next command
detail.notes: Basic capability for command line data stream processing

detail.usage: Write standard output to a file, overwriting existing content
detail.notes: Overwrites existing content in the target file

detail.usage: Append standard output to the end of a file
detail.notes: Suitable for accumulating logs

detail.usage: Use file content as standard input for a command
detail.notes: Useful in batch and text processing scenarios

detail.usage: Start a task in a new window, background or specified program
detail.notes: Can be used to launch multiple local service windows simultaneously

title: VS Code Shortcuts

detail.notes: Ctrl+Shift+L selects all identical words
